USD/CRC · MONEX — Long/Short Quant Report (full history, net of real slippage)

2,663 sessions · 2014-12-08 → 2026-06-19 · execution cost 0.65 CRC/side (~13.7 bps) · long/short USD positions

This revises the earlier 18-month study with 11.5 years of data and your real slippage of 0.65 CRC per side. Both change the conclusion. The 18-month sample was a favourable window with an untradeable VWAP fill; on the full history and real costs, the high-frequency edge does not survive — but a slow trend-following long/short does, with a believable net Sharpe near 1.

  • REVISION — the earlier "Sharpe ~5" was a mirage. It came from an 18-month window (the colón only became signal-rich after 2018) and from marking P&L at the VWAP, which you cannot actually trade at.
  • DEAD — daily order-flow loses money net of your cost. The signal is real (gross next-day Sharpe 2.8, positive every year since 2017), but it flips position ~95.2×/yr and the ~5 bps/trade edge is smaller than your ~13.7 bps/side slippage → net Sharpe -2.11. It only pays if you are the liquidity provider, not the taker.
  • SURVIVES — a slow trend-following long/short USD. Donchian-40 breakout: net Sharpe 1.04, 2.5 round-trips/yr, +452 bps/yr, max drawdown -12.9%. Both the long-USD and short-USD legs make money; permutation p=0.0.
  • CAVEAT — it only earns when the colón trends. In-sample (2015–21, mostly the pegged years) net Sharpe was just 0.28; out-of-sample (2021–26, two-way regimes) 1.83. If the BCCR re-pins the rate, trend-following goes flat — it won't bleed, but it won't earn. Managed-float tail risk remains the dominant danger.

Part D · Verdict & deployment

Is it worth pursuing?

  • As a standalone speculative book: marginal. Net Sharpe ~1 with ~13% drawdowns and a known tail risk is not compelling on its own, and you need an instrument that lets you be long and short USD/CRC at 0.65 CRC slippage.
  • As a treasury / hedging overlay (most likely your case): yes, useful. If you already run USD/CRC exposure, a slow trend filter that tells you when to be long vs short USD — trading only a few times a year — is a cheap, robust improvement over a static hedge, and it survived 11.5 years and real costs.
  • Do NOT deploy the daily order-flow signal as a taker. It is real but you will pay it all away in slippage. The only way to monetise it is as a market-maker / internaliser capturing the spread you are currently paying.
  • Dominant risk: the BCCR. A re-peg flattens the trend edge; a policy break / intervention is the fat tail this sample can't price. Size for survival, keep the lookback slow, and re-estimate continuously.
Caveats. P&L is net of your stated 0.65 CRC/side slippage but gross of financing/borrow and any market impact; the colón is not freely shortable for all participants. Tests assume you can hold a signed USD position at MONEX. Your slippage example described price improvement (buy below / sell above spot); this report models the economically standard adverse 0.65 CRC — tell me if you meant otherwise and the cost layer flips. The "best board offers" block in the export was empty, so VWAP / close are the price proxies.

Reproducible: parse_monex → analyze → eda → strategies → backtest → build_report, then weasyprint for the PDF. Strategy numbers are read live from backtest_results.json.
